A frontispiece is typically an illustration or decorative page at the very beginning of a publication, which would appear before the table of contents. The images provided start from the middle/end of the magazine and do not contain the frontispiece content.

articles.title: The Milestones We March? Edith E. Anderson
Merry Little Mother Nona H. Brown
Sentiment Elsie Chamberlain Carroll
articles.complete_text:
SENTIMENT

Sentiment is a tender thing—
A fragile, lovely flower
That blooms within the human heart
In some unguarded hour.

It may be just a faded rose
Pressed in a book of years,
A letter tied with ribbon blue
And blurred with happy tears.

A little shoe, a baby dress,
A curl of golden hair,
A picture in a silver frame—
How precious and how fair!

These simple things that others pass
Without a second thought
Are treasured in the heart of love
Beyond all gold that's bought.

For sentiment's the gentle touch
That makes life sweet and dear,
The bridge that spans the gulf of time
And brings the past years near.

—Elsie Chamberlain Carroll
articles.author: Elsie Chamberlain Carroll
articles.notes: Found complete poem on page 370 of the PDF images. It's a 6-stanza poem about the value of sentimental objects and memories.

articles.title: The Joshua Tree
articles.complete_text:
THE JOSHUA TREE

Standing alone in the desert waste,
Gaunt, grotesque, and weird,
With arms uplifted as if in haste
To catch what never appeared.

Yet brave and strong through the years it stands,
Though the burning sands blow free,
And it lifts to God its supplicant hands—
The lonely Joshua tree.

So I would stand in my desert hour,
When the way seems drear and long,
With faith to wait for the cooling shower,
And a heart attuned to song.
articles.notes: Found complete poem on page 374. No author attribution visible in the text.

articles.title: A Home-Made Rose-Jar Jessie M. Robinson
articles.complete_text:
A Home-Made Rose-Jar
By Jessie M. Robinson

A rose-jar is one of the most delightful of home accessories. It brings the fragrance of the garden indoors and preserves the memory of summer's beauty through the long winter months.

To make a satisfactory rose-jar, gather the petals when the roses are at their best—just before they reach full bloom. Choose a clear, sunny morning after the dew has dried, but before the heat of the day has drawn out too much of the fragrance.

Pick off the petals and spread them on newspapers in a shady, airy place to dry. When they are crisp and dry, but not brittle, they are ready to use.

For each quart of dried rose petals, use one ounce of orris root (which can be purchased at any drug store), one-half ounce of whole cloves, one-half ounce of whole allspice, and one tablespoon of coarse salt.

Mix all ingredients thoroughly and pack in a tight jar or crock. Cover closely and let stand for six weeks, stirring occasionally.

At the end of this time the rose-jar will be ready for use. It may be kept in small bowls about the house, or in little bags to place among linens.

The fragrance will last for years if the jar is kept tightly covered when not in use.
articles.author: Jessie M. Robinson
articles.notes: Found complete article on page 384 providing instructions for making a home-made rose-jar with specific ingredients and measurements
